refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows design parameters (e.g., hydraulic response time, travel speed envelope, load handling efficiency) to be evaluated independently of operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, load handling, operator interface, autonomy/tele-operation) do not introduce delays that prevent the cycle from completing within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time across multiple runs; confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. Separately validate that operator training, maintenance state, and site logistics do not degrade performance below acceptance threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the load phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel from load site to dump site within the travel phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable load release and return to load site within the dump phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned idle time due to equipment malfunction or control latency.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across phases, with real-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment to maintain p95 compliance.

DP3: Design a predictive cycle-time model that accounts for site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) and pre-computes optimal phase parameters before cycle execution, with fallback to nominal timing.

DP4: Establish a closed-loop feedback control system that measures actual phase durations against budgeted targets and applies corrective actions (speed modulation, path optimization) within each phase.

DP5: Decouple cycle-time performance from external variability (site conditions, load variance) by introducing a buffering or scheduling layer that absorbs variance and guarantees deterministic phase transitions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 (integrated): Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across load, travel, and dump phases with explicit phase-duration targets. Pair this with closed-loop feedback control that monitors actual phase durations in real-time, detects deviations from budget, and applies corrective actions (e.g., speed adjustment, path re-optimization) to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability. This design separates budget planning (DP2) from execution control (DP4), enabling independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-duration targets and site-condition models: if terrain/load prediction is inaccurate, budget allocation fails. Mitigation: use conservative (pessimistic) initial budgets and adaptive re-allocation.

Feedback control loop responsiveness vs. phase duration: if corrective actions (e.g., speed change) have long latency relative to phase duration, control may be ineffective. Mitigation: ensure actuation bandwidth is ≥ 3× fastest phase duration.

Cross-phase dependencies: dump phase may be blocked if travel phase overruns; load phase may be blocked if dump phase is slow. Mitigation: design phases as independent state machines with explicit handoff protocols.
Measurement coupling: if cycle-time sensors are unreliable or have high latency, feedback control becomes unstable. Mitigation: specify sensor accuracy and latency requirements explicitly.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP4 avoids monolithic cycle design and supports independent optimization of allocation vs. control. However, success depends critically on accurate site-condition modeling and low-latency feedback. DP1 (phase decomposition) is a prerequisite; DP3 (predictive modeling) is a supporting enabler.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) are measurable or predictable with sufficient accuracy to inform initial budget allocation. RISK: If site variability is high and unpredictable, budget allocation will be conservative, reducing throughput. MITIGATION: Implement adaptive re-allocation based on first few cycles.

ASSUMPTION: Actuation system (drive motors, hydraulics, steering) can respond to corrective commands (speed, path changes) with latency ≤ 10% of phase duration. RISK: If latency is high, feedback control cannot correct deviations within a single phase. MITIGATION: Specify actuation bandwidth in DP realization; consider predictive control if latency is unavoidable.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time measurement (via sensors, GPS, or odometry) is accurate to ±5% of CT_target. RISK: If measurement noise is high, feedback control will oscillate or diverge. MITIGATION: Implement low-pass filtering and deadband logic in feedback loop.

ASSUMPTION: Load, travel, and dump phases can be executed sequentially without blocking or resource contention. RISK: If phases share actuators or hydraulic circuits, phase transitions may be delayed. MITIGATION: Validate phase independence in DP realization; design hydraulic/electrical architecture to support concurrent actuation if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined CT_target is achievable under stated duty cycle and site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic (e.g., requires speeds exceeding safety limits), no DP can satisfy FR. MITIGATION: Validate CT_target feasibility during requirements review; document assumptions about load mass, distance, and terrain.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory speed limits that make CT_target unachievable). RISK: Safety constraints may force conservative phase durations. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety team early; ensure DP respects all safety constraints.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range of site conditions (load mass, distance, terrain type) under which p95 ≤ CT_target must hold; (2) the measurement method and accuracy for cycle-time validation; (3) the priority order if CT_target conflicts with safety or operational constraints. Decompose DP2+DP4 into two separate FRs: FR2a (allocate cycle-time budget across phases) and FR2b (control phase durations in real-time to maintain budget compliance). This separation clarifies ownership and enables independent testing of budget allocation vs. feedback control. Add explicit IM layer: IM2a (cycle-time budget module, owned by planning/scheduling team) and IM2b (feedback control module, owned by vehicle control team), with a well-defined interface (phase-duration targets and actual measurements).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle-time budget allocation engine and closed-loop feedback control system that owns budget planning (DP2), real-time phase-duration monitoring (DP4), deviation detection, and corrective action orchestration across load, travel, and dump phases.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives actual phase durations, vehicle speed, position, load state, and site conditions from Vehicle in real-time to enable closed-loop feedback control and deviation detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends corrective action commands (speed adjustments, path re-optimization requests, phase-duration targets) to Vehicle and Teleoperation to maintain cycle-time budget compliance.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; sends corrective action proposals for safety-aware feedback control tuning.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication backbone for telemetry ingestion and tasking command dispatch in remote/autonomous operation scenarios.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for mission-level optimization logic (budget allocation, cycle-time control, corrective action orchestration). Budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) are both algorithmic/decision-making functions that belong in the planning/optimization layer. Minimal diffusion: telemetry ingestion and tasking dispatch are well-defined external interfaces; safety constraints are handled via SafetyAlert interface. No duplication of control logic across Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a planning/control orchestration layer. Placing budget allocation and feedback control logic in Vehicle would couple mission-level optimization to hardware specifics, making tuning and reuse difficult. Corrective actions (speed, path) must be commanded to Vehicle, not owned by it. High diffusion: budget logic would leak into teleoperation and safety services for coordination.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer, not a planning/optimization engine. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are mission-level concerns that should not reside in the operator interface. Placing DP2+DP4 here would scatter control logic across Vehicle and Safety Services. High diffusion and poor separation of concerns.

Weak fit. Safety Services owns constraint enforcement and hazard detection, not cycle-time optimization. While feedback control must respect safety constraints (via SafetyAlert interface), the budget allocation and corrective action logic belong in the mission-planning layer (NGHE Solution). Placing DP2+DP4 here would conflate safety assurance with performance optimization, reducing modularity and testability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal boundary for DP2+DP4 because it is the designated mission-planning and optimization layer. Budget allocation (DP2) is a planning-time decision that distributes CT_target across phases; closed-loop feedback control (DP4) is a runtime optimization function that monitors and corrects deviations. Both are algorithmic/decision-making functions that operate at the mission level, independent of vehicle hardware or operator interface. Containment is strong because the module already owns mission-level logic; external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cleanly separate concerns and enable independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness without diffusion into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: If site variability (e.g., terrain, congestion) is not adequately captured in Telemetry, feedback control may fail to detect true deviations. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface includes high-fidelity phase-duration and environmental data.

Risk: Corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) may conflict with safety constraints or vehicle capabilities. Mitigation: Implement SafetyAlert feedback loop and validate corrective action feasibility before dispatch via Tasking.

Risk: Network latency in remote operation may delay telemetry ingestion or tasking command delivery, reducing feedback control responsiveness. Mitigation: Design feedback control with latency tolerance; use Network Link interface to monitor and adapt control gains.

Risk: Budget allocation strategy (DP2) may be overly rigid if site conditions change dynamically. Mitigation: Implement adaptive budget re-allocation logic triggered by Telemetry anomalies or SafetyAlert signals.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (actual phase durations). NGHE Solution owns the closed-loop feedback control (DP4) that monitors and reports p95 compliance in real-time. Verification can be automated through telemetry log analysis and statistical aggregation across multiple cycles. Site conditions are captured as inputs to the feedback control loop, enabling conditional acceptance testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR requirement is decomposed into two distinct design parameters (DP2: budget allocation planning; DP4: closed-loop feedback control) that together address the full cycle-time management problem. Budget allocation (DP2) ensures initial phase-duration targets are rational and feasible; feedback control (DP4) ensures runtime deviations are detected and corrected. Both DPs are owned by NGHE Solution, eliminating ambiguity about responsibility for cycle-time compliance.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2+DP4 (integrated) directly and sufficiently addresses FR. DP2 (centralized budget allocation) solves the planning-time problem of distributing CT_target across load, travel, dump phases with explicit targets. DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) solves the runtime problem of detecting deviations and applying corrective actions. The pairing is non-redundant: budget allocation is a one-time or periodic planning decision; feedback control is continuous execution-time monitoring and correction. Together they form a complete control architecture (plan → execute → monitor → correct) that logically satisfies the FR. No gaps or logical inconsistencies detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface must include high-fidelity phase-duration attribution (load start/end, travel start/end, dump start/end timestamps) and environmental context (terrain type, congestion, load mass). If Telemetry provides only aggregate cycle time without phase granularity, feedback control cannot isolate which phase is causing deviation, reducing corrective action precision. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry schema to include phase-duration vectors and site-condition tags.

Risk: Corrective action commands (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) are dispatched via Tasking interface to Vehicle and Teleoperation. If Vehicle or Teleoperation interpret or modify these commands without NGHE Solution awareness, feedback control loop is broken. Mitigation: Implement command acknowledgment and execution confirmation via Tasking interface; log all corrective actions for closed-loop validation.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional (inout). If Safety Services rejects a corrective action proposed by NGHE Solution, the feedback control loop must have a fallback strategy (e.g., reduce speed further, defer cycle, escalate to operator). If fallback logic is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution, corrective action orchestration becomes diffused. Mitigation: Define explicit safety-aware corrective action hierarchy and fallback policies within NGHE Solution; document SafetyAlert rejection handling.

Risk: Network Link latency in remote operation may cause telemetry ingestion delay or tasking command delivery delay. If feedback control assumes zero latency, corrective actions may be stale or ineffective. Mitigation: Implement latency-aware feedback control with predictive phase-duration estimation; monitor Network Link quality via telemetry and adapt control gains dynamically.

Risk: Budget allocation strategy (DP2) is described as 'independent tuning' from feedback control (DP4), but no explicit interface or trigger for budget re-allocation is defined. If site conditions change dynamically (e.g., congestion increases), budget targets may become infeasible. Mitigation: Define adaptive budget re-allocation logic triggered by Telemetry anomalies (e.g., sustained phase-duration increase) or SafetyAlert signals; document re-allocation frequency and constraints.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and optimal home for DP2+DP4 because: (1) Budget allocation is a mission-level planning function that requires knowledge of CT_target, phase dependencies, and site constraints—all mission-level concerns. (2) Closed-loop feedback control is a runtime optimization function that must operate independently of vehicle hardware specifics or operator interface—a planning/optimization layer responsibility. (3) Corrective action orchestration (speed, path, phase-duration targets) is a decision-making function that must be centralized to avoid conflicting commands to Vehicle or Teleoperation. (4) External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and cleanly separate concerns: NGHE Solution receives data (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and sends commands (Tasking), without owning Vehicle execution, Teleoperation UI, or Safety Services constraint enforcement. No duplication of control logic across modules. Minimal diffusion: all algorithmic/decision-making aspects of DP2+DP4 are contained within NGHE Solution; execution and constraint enforcement are delegated to appropriate external modules via interfaces.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Enhance Telemetry interface specification to mandate phase-duration attribution (load, travel, dump phase timestamps with ±tolerance), environmental context (terrain type, congestion level, load mass, vehicle speed profile), and deviation flags (e.g., 'phase_duration_exceeded_budget'). This ensures feedback control has sufficient fidelity to isolate root causes and apply targeted corrective actions.

Revision 2: Define explicit SafetyAlert rejection handling policy within NGHE Solution. Document fallback corrective action hierarchy (e.g., if speed reduction is rejected, defer cycle; if path re-optimization is rejected, escalate to operator). Ensure all fallback paths are owned by NGHE Solution and logged for audit.

Revision 3: Specify Tasking interface to include command acknowledgment and execution confirmation. Define timeout and retry logic for corrective action commands. Implement closed-loop validation: NGHE Solution must receive confirmation that Vehicle executed speed adjustment or Teleoperation dispatched path re-optimization before considering corrective action complete.

Revision 4: Document adaptive budget re-allocation logic as part of DP2. Define trigger conditions (e.g., sustained phase-duration increase >10% over N cycles, SafetyAlert constraint change), re-allocation frequency (e.g., per-cycle, per-shift), and constraints (e.g., do not reduce phase-duration targets below vehicle capability limits). Specify interface or internal state machine for budget re-allocation within NGHE Solution.

Revision 5: Add Network Link latency tolerance specification to feedback control design (DP4). Define maximum acceptable latency for telemetry ingestion and tasking command delivery. Implement predictive phase-duration estimation to compensate for latency. Monitor Network Link quality via telemetry and adapt feedback control gains dynamically (e.g., reduce control responsiveness if latency exceeds threshold).

Revision 6: Clarify the separation of concerns between DP2 (budget allocation planning) and DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) by documenting their interaction model. Specify: (a) When is budget allocation executed (e.g., at mission start, per-shift, per-cycle)? (b) How does feedback control signal to DP2 that budget targets are infeasible (e.g., via Telemetry anomaly or internal state)? (c) What is the priority if DP2 and DP4 conflict (e.g., budget says reduce speed, but feedback control says increase speed to meet deadline)? Document decision logic within NGHE Solution.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR-DP-IM alignment is logically sound (all three gates pass), but the diffusion_risks and recommended_revisions reveal critical gaps in DP specification clarity and interface contracts that must be resolved before decomposition proceeds. The six recommended revisions are not minor refinements—they address: (1) Telemetry schema sufficiency for feedback control fidelity, (2) SafetyAlert rejection handling and fallback orchestration, (3) Tasking interface command confirmation and closed-loop validation, (4) Adaptive budget re-allocation triggering and constraints, (5) Network Link latency tolerance and predictive compensation, and (6) Explicit interaction model between DP2 and DP4. These gaps create implementation risk and ambiguity in ownership boundaries. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without resolving these DP-level specifications would propagate uncertainty into lower levels and complicate verification. Revise DP2 and DP4 specifications to incorporate recommended revisions, then re-gate before deriving sub-FRs.

revise.why: DP2 (budget allocation planning) and DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) require enhanced specification to address: (a) Telemetry interface schema and phase-duration attribution requirements; (b) SafetyAlert rejection handling and fallback corrective action hierarchy; (c) Tasking interface command acknowledgment and execution confirmation protocol; (d) Adaptive budget re-allocation logic, triggers, and constraints; (e) Network Link latency tolerance and predictive compensation strategy; (f) Explicit interaction model and priority resolution between DP2 and DP4. These revisions must be documented in DP specifications before sub-FR decomposition to ensure lower-level requirements inherit clear, unambiguous design intent and interface contracts.

assumptions: Telemetry interface will be enhanced to provide phase-duration vectors (load, travel, dump timestamps) and environmental context (terrain, congestion, load mass) with sufficient fidelity for feedback control to isolate root causes of cycle-time deviations.

Tasking interface will implement command acknowledgment and execution confirmation protocol, enabling NGHE Solution to validate that corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) are executed by Vehicle or Teleoperation as intended.

SafetyAlert interface rejection of corrective actions is expected and will be handled by explicit fallback logic owned by NGHE Solution (e.g., reduce speed further, defer cycle, escalate to operator).
Network Link latency is bounded and predictable enough to enable latency-aware feedback control with predictive phase-duration estimation and dynamic gain adaptation.

Budget allocation (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) operate on compatible timescales: budget allocation is periodic (per-mission, per-shift, or per-cycle); feedback control is continuous (per-phase or per-cycle). Interaction model and priority resolution are defined.

NGHE Solution has authority to dispatch corrective action commands via Tasking interface and to receive telemetry and safety alerts without requiring approval from Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services (except SafetyAlert rejection handling).
open_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface schema is not yet finalized. If phase-duration attribution is missing or coarse-grained, feedback control cannot isolate which phase (load, travel, dump) is causing cycle-time deviation, reducing corrective action precision and effectiveness. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry schema in DP4 revision; validate schema with Vehicle and Telemetry providers before implementation.

Risk: SafetyAlert rejection handling is not explicitly defined. If NGHE Solution proposes a corrective action (e.g., speed increase) and Safety Services rejects it, the feedback control loop must have a fallback strategy. If fallback logic is not owned by NGHE Solution, corrective action orchestration becomes diffused and unpredictable. Mitigation: Define explicit fallback hierarchy in DP4 revision; document all fallback paths and ownership.

Risk: Tasking interface command confirmation protocol is not specified. If Vehicle or Teleoperation execute corrective actions without acknowledgment or confirmation, NGHE Solution cannot validate closed-loop correctness. Corrective actions may be silently ignored or modified. Mitigation: Specify command acknowledgment and execution confirmation in DP4 revision; implement timeout and retry logic.

Risk: Adaptive budget re-allocation logic is not defined. If site conditions change dynamically (e.g., congestion increases, terrain becomes difficult), budget targets may become infeasible. If budget re-allocation is not triggered automatically, cycle-time compliance may degrade. Mitigation: Define adaptive budget re-allocation triggers and constraints in DP2 revision; specify re-allocation frequency and decision logic.

Risk: Network Link latency may cause telemetry ingestion delay or tasking command delivery delay. If feedback control assumes zero latency, corrective actions may be stale or ineffective. Mitigation: Specify latency tolerance and predictive compensation strategy in DP4 revision; monitor Network Link quality and adapt control gains dynamically.

Risk: Interaction model between DP2 and DP4 is not explicit. If budget allocation and feedback control conflict (e.g., budget says reduce speed, feedback control says increase speed), priority and resolution logic are undefined. Mitigation: Document interaction model and priority resolution in DP2 and DP4 revisions; specify decision logic within NGHE Solution.

Risk: High safety criticality requires that all corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization, phase-duration targets) are validated against safety constraints before execution. If SafetyAlert interface is not bidirectional or if rejection handling is incomplete, safety compliance may be compromised. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and rejection handling is explicit and logged; validate all corrective actions against safety constraints before tasking.
evidence_needed: Finalized Telemetry interface schema specification, including phase-duration attribution (load, travel, dump timestamps with tolerance), environmental context (terrain type, congestion level, load mass, vehicle speed profile), and deviation flags. Validation that schema is sufficient for feedback control to isolate root causes of cycle-time deviations.

Explicit SafetyAlert rejection handling policy and fallback corrective action hierarchy, documented in DP4 specification. Examples of fallback paths (e.g., if speed reduction is rejected, defer cycle; if path re-optimization is rejected, escalate to operator). Validation that all fallback paths are owned by NGHE Solution and logged for audit.

Tasking interface command acknowledgment and execution confirmation protocol, including timeout and retry logic. Specification of how NGHE Solution validates that Vehicle executed speed adjustment or Teleoperation dispatched path re-optimization. Validation that closed-loop confirmation is feasible and timely.

Adaptive budget re-allocation logic, including trigger conditions (e.g., sustained phase-duration increase >10% over N cycles, SafetyAlert constraint change), re-allocation frequency (e.g., per-cycle, per-shift), and constraints (e.g., do not reduce phase-duration targets below vehicle capability limits). Specification of interface or internal state machine for budget re-allocation within NGHE Solution.

Network Link latency tolerance specification and predictive phase-duration estimation strategy. Specification of maximum acceptable latency for telemetry ingestion and tasking command delivery. Validation that predictive compensation is feasible and effective. Specification of dynamic gain adaptation logic based on Network Link quality monitoring.

Explicit interaction model between DP2 (budget allocation planning) and DP4 (closed-loop feedback control), including: (a) When is budget allocation executed (e.g., at mission start, per-shift, per-cycle)? (b) How does feedback control signal to DP2 that budget targets are infeasible? (c) What is the priority if DP2 and DP4 conflict? (d) How are conflicts resolved within NGHE Solution? Validation that interaction model is consistent with mission-level requirements and safety constraints.

Verification plan for DP2 and DP4 revisions, including test cases for: (a) Telemetry schema sufficiency (phase-duration attribution accuracy, environmental context completeness); (b) SafetyAlert rejection handling (fallback execution, logging); (c) Tasking interface confirmation (command acknowledgment, execution validation); (d) Adaptive budget re-allocation (trigger detection, re-allocation correctness); (e) Network Link latency compensation (predictive accuracy, gain adaptation); (f) DP2-DP4 interaction (conflict resolution, priority enforcement). Validation that verification is feasible and automated where possible.
